description: Return to the LM and SEP Deployment

tokens: Okay, where did they want it (Meaning the seismic charge) deployed?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It'll be at a range of 0.6, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You've got a block right ahead of you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And remember you'll be taking photos coming back here, Jack...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I got a few going out, Bob, but they weren't too well spaced.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I assume you've got the low-gain antenna aligned.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's got to be Trident, Jack, because that's too big for anything else.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's the classic raindrop pattern over this fine debris.
I'd say that the surface definitely is sorted: the fine regolithic material forming one fraction and then the blocks another.
The blocks are probably...Those blocks greater than 2 centimeters in diameter, in general, make up (that is, cover) less than 10 percent of the surface.
But there are some big ones, fairly uniformly distributed.
There are blocks a meter in diameter.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Jack, that big crater out there at 2 o'clock (relative to his current heading, which is roughly north) has probably got to be Sherlock.
That's got to be Sherlock over there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think the only place I've really identified that we can go to is to Station 6.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, but we've got to get on a high vantage point here one of these days.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Well, I think we'll find Camelot without any problem.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, I'd rather straddle or go through those little ones.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Bob, here's another crater about the same size we sampled (at) the last station.
And it doesn't have as many blocks, but it does have blocks.
And from this distance, their vesicular texture and their light color shows up very well.
I suspect they're the same general kind.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You got a range and bearing, there, guys, please.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Did you take a picture, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're pointed right at Station 6, I think, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think you may be right.
I just want to get up here where I can (garbled)...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Not the one on the (boulder) track but the one over there to the right of that.
(Pause) Unless the one with the track...I've got mixed emotions (as to) which is 6.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Look over there to the left.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That looks like the same kind of rock except it doesn't have any vesicles.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's some white stuff in that rock.
Just let me take a quick pic (static, probably as Gene turns).
See that one right in front of us?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, you mean this one, here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, that's a big zap pit, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Same rock with big zap pits.
(Pause) I've got to change the (focus)...I was too close.
Although there are...I think those are zap pits.
It's a little hard to say.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Looks like a big chip out of the rock.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They're white halos; it just has more of them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But it's a big one; it's about an inch and a half or 2 inches across.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I tell you, I've got to go and get my size and geometry squared away.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, we're moving at about 11 clicks (kilometers per hour) right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Remember the charge goes off at 0.6.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Can you look to your left?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Call it a slide or not, but that's the white mantle.
That's my first real good picture (means 'view') of it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I got some of that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't want to go in that crater, that's what I don't want to do.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got a couple of shots right out in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me get my...Okay, I got my "locator".
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, now (for) this one we want me to get a partial pan until something's identified.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We've got to turn that way anyway.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I copy that as charge number 7.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And we'd like a frame number when you get done there, Jack, after you get it (the charge) on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, start a pan around it, Gene...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, let me get it out...Okay, I'm going to start slowly around it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Static) (Garbled) wheels cleared it by...got to be a lot.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is my low gain dropping out?
(Pause) How much are my wheels missing it by, going around?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The low gain is set again.
(Pause) Okay, we're heading on back to SEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, and a frame count there, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, the pan was more or less complete at 146.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, you know, the more I look at this - (To Gene) watch out for those babies there - at this dark dust, if you will, the more it doesn't seem like the kind of thing you'd expect to have been derived from the underlying bedrock.
But I think you're going to have to play that game in the lab right now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'll see how it works out later.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It just seems dark and much too fine grain.
You don't have the impression that you're getting the size distribution you'd expect to get by having all these blocks around.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (There is) definitely, I think, at least in my mind, two size populations...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, that almost looks like bedrock exposed (over) in there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, why don't you take a pass over that way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, I can get through there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you know where you are?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's pretty steep down in there.
I'm not sure I want to drive down there yet.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No, I didn't mean down in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And how about a range and bearing when you stop to take the picture.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I get a distinct impression that, as Jack says, it's going to be hard to tell whether this is regolith composed (means "derived") from the rock field we see around, but you can see that dark mantle over on top of almost all the rocks.
Except we have fresh glass, possibly, in the bottom of some of these small craters.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Everywhere else there is actually mantle, I believe, in and around some of the crevices and in the vesicles and what have you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's all material, though, that could be knocked in there by the local impact.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; but I gather you find a lot of material on top of the rocks.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Gene) I think we lost them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They're not nearly as covered with dust as they get when you drop one.
It's just really a salting or a scattering of debris in the depressions...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The projections of the rock are perfectly clean.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah; but, except where rocks within the craters are evident, the crater (rims and walls) are subtly covered over with this mantle.
You don't see any good sharp ridges or walls on some of these craters.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Man, I tell you, you could lose the rear end of this thing in a hurry, if you'd like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think you have lost a fender.
I keep getting rained on here (with lunar dust).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Look what's ahead of us here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can see it in the shadow.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh boy, that's going to be terrible.
That is really going to be bad.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think I know when because I just started to notice it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, I'm going to state what Gene said (at 122:47:13) slightly differently.
There just aren't a lot of very sharp, bright craters, but there are some.
All the craters seem to be pretty well formed.
Matter of fact, for example, hasn't filled the...(To Gene) Are we due to deploy that thing now?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, I want to come in at a (heading directly west toward the SEP transmitter)...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Finishing his thought) Hasn't filled the bottom of the craters.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to come in at a heading here and see if I can get on it for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Static as Gene turns the Rover and loses low-gain pointing)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Look at the dust it's produced.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, it's going to make things...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't know how to keep that thing on.
Okay, I'm rolling west right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, I don't like losing that fender.
(Pause) We're back at the SEP, Bob.
I'm starting to lay out my first track.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Jack) Okay, can you get out?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're about 5 minutes behind on the arrival time at the SEP.
And I assume that the range and bearing, when you got there, was about zero.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Gene) Okay, get...Let me leave my camera.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Responding to Bob) Let me read it.
252 (bearing to the place where Gene last reset the indicator), 2.5 (distance driven since the start of the traverse), and 0 (range to the last reset point).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Apparently misreading the frame counter) And the LMP frame count is 197, and it was still turning.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Amp-hours are 108 105, and batteries (temperatures) are 100 and 120.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I need my camera, don't I?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't think it's much good to you with a (frame count of) 197 there, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, you take the pictures (as per
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The location is in about the least cratered area I could find, between a large crater or a large depression that ranges from maybe 50 to 150 meters behind (that is, east of) the LM - that's maybe east-southeast - and it (the SEP)'s between that depression and another large depression that is really a doublet with a blocky septum between them.
(Brief static as Gene turns) That's to the northeast of the LM about 200 meters; that's the start of that second depression.
I think we can get a nice layout, although there'll be a general slope, I believe, towards the LM, of about a degree.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, that's no real problem, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Jack, am I about abeam of you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I'll turn in around this (small) crater.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, if you come right...(Long burst of static as Gene turns, an indication that Houston hasn't switched to feeding comm through the LM)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Static clears as Gene re-aims the low-gain antenna) That depression to the northeast is at least a couple hundred meters in diameter, and it's joined with one that's probably of comparable size just to the northwest of the first depression.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that too, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Static; garbled) we head on to Station 2 without that fender and are we going to be full of dust.
Okay, there's no special...I can park 180 but...(Long Pause) Okay, Bob, I've stopped, back at the SEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This thing is a lot harder to turn than it was in training.
Okay, that's the first two we've got to deploy.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, do you want me to dust here?
(No answer) I'll dust back at the LM.
We're going to deploy the SEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Frustrated by the trouble he's having with the reels) God bless!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They all say to dust, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Jack) You're going to have to wait for me, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I've got antennas all over the place here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, as a matter of fact, you do.
I'll make it (the dusting job) a quick one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No, I can handle it, I think, here.
But we're going to need your tongs to pick them up and not get them all confused.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Looking at his checklist) Okay, I'll deploy number 2 and 4.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: So this one must be number 2.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Going to the front of the Rover) Let me run around and dust!
(Pause) Let me tell you, this dust isn't going to be fun tomorrow!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I won't bore you with details on why you see the antenna all over the ground (chuckles), but it has to do with one-sixth g.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Understand you dropped a couple of the antenna reels.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You know, Bob, they're very...(Responding to Bob) More accurately, I dropped three of them.
Bob, you know this fine-grained dust that we're in could be ground-up pyroclastic.
It might grind more easily than other things, and the blocks are those that have been excavated from below that pyroclastic by the larger craters and some of the smaller ones in the area.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Responding to Jack's pyroclastic theory) Roger, Jack.
That would make a nice story, wouldn't it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, you'd think glassy pyroclastic might turn into regolith a little bit faster than some of these other things.
But we'll check that one out.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Jack, did you get the reels straightened out again?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: How can you stop a crew like this?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Geno, I'm on my way.
Pull gently on that thing (the SEP transmitter) because it's awful easy to knock it over.
I had that geophone module all over the place.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can't tell whether I'm pulling gently or not.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Any time you feel a tug, stop.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: At least we're pulling at the base.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I better watch what I'm backing into.
There's a lot of holes around here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm glad we Velcroed those tabs.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I'm at the end, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, what happened was (that) which we thought might happen.
It twisted on my...I'll be there in just a second.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I want f/11 at 1/250 (of a second exposure) and 74 feet (focus).
Ah, ah, yes; you're pulling it over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I've got all sorts of slack in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, let me back up a skosh and take the slack out.
And I would say, offhand...Oh, boy.
That's about as close to a one-sixth-g orthogonal...at least, not orthogonal yet, but straight-lined.
Stay there, and I'll take a picture.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, if you try and stick that thing (the reel) in (the ground), Jack, you're going to fall over.
Just set it down, and we'll stay away from it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That was a good idea, but...That's a good straight line.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Singing) We were strolling in the (pause) Taurus one day...Well, we've had lots of good ideas in our time.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, boy; the thing that makes me sick is losing that fender.
I can stand a lot of things, but I sure don't like that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is that the one on the ground?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's probably the one on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Take it; doesn't make any difference.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I'm deploying the...The LMP's deploying reel 3 for your photography purposes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Turning to face Gene) Hey, push that (?) in.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pleased with himself) I found a brown rock that I'm going to bring back!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think it's the back side of a piece of glass, but it's brown.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Looking back) Well, I think I'm more or less on your (Rover) track.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, we're...(To himself) Oh, oh, oh, ooh, stop, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Every time I do something, I change the setting.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I straightened the line out a little bit better after I took the picture...(It had) a few kinks in it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I saw it when I was driving with the Rover!
I knew I'd be able to come back here because of the tracks.
Looks like an old piece of bread.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is that the one that came out of the hatch?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Hearing Bob, not remembering the piece of bread that had flown out the hatch when they first opened it) Huh?
Well, it's a piece of glass, all right, part of it crumbled.
I got to get that in a bag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, man, is that a nice piece of glass.
Just laying out there all by itself.
Jack, you got a bag handy while I take my pan?
I can't reach a bag; I got this sample in the wrong hand.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You don't have...Well, take one off of mine and give it to me.
I'll take it back to the Rover.
Watch it; you've got a wire under your foot.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It was about half way out on the north course...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...about halfway out on the north course of the SEP.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sort of a yellow-brown, as a matter of fact.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, it (the checklist) says (Pause) "take locator photo to LM".
I thought I took a pan here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, the "locator" is really all you need; but a partial pan to show the area would be appreciated.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm going to get a partial pan, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Kidding Bob) The only reason I'm doing it is I know it would be appreciated.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Reading) "Take locator to photo LM." I got it (in the pan).
Bob, I'm on (frame count)...If I can get where I can read it...on about 71 on my frame count; and let me give you...Boy, we got to stay out of this area, Jack.
We'll pick up these cables (with our feet) just as sure as the devil.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Houston, will you look at your solar panels?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You notice how they flop up?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think we need some gray tape.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I got that one, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Returning to the Rover) The wires have memory.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I need gray tape (to hold them flat).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me put this in your bag, and I'll get the gray tape.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We didn't exactly break any records collecting samples, but at least we got an ALSEP deployed.
That gray tape, Jack, is not going to stick on anything with dust.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I know; that's what I was thinking.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Because I just been there with that fender.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just like the cover of the...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You want to take those solar panels off?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me hold on to you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: If not, let's take them off and hold them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No, I think it's going to be easier this way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't think they (the solar panels) are going to be much problem the way they are, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, go ahead and lean if you want.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know how long it (the tape) will stay.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's only going to stay for 2 days, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: If it holds until we see it again...(at the start of EVA-2, before they plan to take any SEP data).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Garbled) Don't knock the whole thing over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) The piece of tape is so dusty it may not work.
(Pause) Okay, are you happy with the alignment?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is the (SEP transmitter) gnomon on the zero mark?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gnomon is right up the zero mark.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's where it's supposed to be.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and the level bubble is just touching the inner circle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) And we have that transmitter switch in Standby, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, there's just a couple little boulders around the transmitter, is that...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, looking where the LM is, if I were you, I'd just walk down.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Boy, that's the key around here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Everything on this SEP is coming off harder than it did when we deployed it at the Cape.
It may not be harder, but it lifted.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now, I got to reorient it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now let me look at it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It settled back just the same.
Zero gnomon and inner circle (for the level bubble).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You want to walk back or ride?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Going to the Rover) It's up to you but...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got to make a new fender tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, let's see, I guess I'm supposed to...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Gene, I presume that the fender that came off is the fender that came off before, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: My tape didn't hold; it was too dusty.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Joking) Hey, watch out for this antenna line I found out here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Bob, I'm not going to change anything right now except get in and travel.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You'll have a nominal close-out, guys, as soon as you get back.
We're right about on the time that we've been figuring on for you guys to get back there on.
Right now, you are 6 hours and 11 minutes into the EVA.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I learned a lot today, let me tell you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Bob) Okay, you're going to lose TV because the high gain is going to be out of whack here in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I got a football-size rock of this coarsely vesicular gabbro, Bob.
It's off a large 3- to 4-meter buried boulder to the north...Oh, let's say, northeast of the LM about 30 meters.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Read you loud and clear on that one.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It'll be in the big bag.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's roughly tabular, 15 by 25 centimeters and about 5 to 7 centimeters thick.
One face is very flat; looks like it was off of a parting plane, which were (sic) in that rock.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and if it fits in the SRC with all the other samples, you might put it there because the SRC's going to be kind of empty.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Got any new (Rover) parking angles for your batteries or anything?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: No, it will be a heading of 013, which is hardly a change at all from the 012 in the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Jack, did you copy my comments about putting that thing (the football-sized sample of the boulder) in the SRC perhaps?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It's in the big bag now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I'd get the other samples - the small ones and particular(ly the) soils - in the SRC first.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, I'm going right about here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Bob, 086 (bearing to SEP), 0.5 (distance driven since resetting, mostly in laying out the antenna pattern), 0.1 (range to SEP), 108, 102 (amp hours).
Amp-hours are at 108, 102; volts are 74 and 75.
Motors are all off scale low, all four of them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To himself) I can't read this thing, because it's full of dust, so I've got to get off and dust it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
